refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety; therefore, safety-driven unavailability is acceptable and must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime events, root causes, timestamps) from deployed NGHE units over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Scheduled Time − Unplanned Downtime) / Total Scheduled Time. Stratify by failure mode (mechanical, electrical, software, safety-triggered) to identify improvement priorities. Conduct independent audit of telemetry logs and maintenance records.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all availability-affecting events (failures, maintenance, safety shutdowns) with timestamp and root cause classification.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance, redundancy, and rapid fault recovery mechanisms.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance personnel.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain safety-driven unavailability as a distinct category, not penalizing availability KPI when safety shutdowns occur.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and reporting system that aggregates telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules; calculate availability metrics in real-time; trigger alerts when availability trends fall below target; enable predictive maintenance scheduling based on component health indicators.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules independently; each module reports its own uptime; aggregate at the NGHE Solution level using a weighted availability formula; no centralized predictive maintenance.

DP-C: Implement redundancy at critical subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual safety processors); design for graceful degradation so that single-point failures do not cause total unavailability; couple with a health monitoring system that detects degraded-mode operation and triggers maintenance alerts.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A (Centralized Availability Monitoring & Predictive Maintenance): Implement a dedicated availability management subsystem within the NGHE Solution that continuously aggregates telemetry from Vehicle (mechanical/electrical health), Teleoperation (network/control availability), and Safety Services (safety-driven shutdown events). The subsystem shall calculate real-time availability metrics, distinguish safety-driven unavailability from failure-driven unavailability, maintain a rolling 30/90-day availability history, and trigger predictive maintenance recommendations when component health indicators predict imminent failure. This DP directly addresses the KPI requirement and enables proactive intervention to sustain ≥0.97 availability.

potential_sources: DP-A depends on reliable telemetry feeds from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; if these modules do not emit consistent, timestamped health events, the availability calculation will be inaccurate. Mitigation: define a standardized telemetry schema (Telemetry interface) that all modules must conform to.

DP-A's predictive maintenance recommendations must be actionable by Maintenance personnel; if the NGHE Solution does not provide a clear maintenance scheduling interface, recommendations will be ignored. Mitigation: couple DP-A with a maintenance task management UI accessible to Maintenance/Quality stakeholder.

Safety-driven shutdowns must be correctly classified to avoid penalizing availability; this requires tight coupling between Safety Services and the availability monitoring subsystem. Mitigation: define a SafetyAlert interface that explicitly tags shutdown events as safety-driven vs. failure-driven.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A is moderately coupled to the Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules via the Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. This is acceptable because these are well-defined, asynchronous event streams. The coupling is not circular and does not create a dependency loop. DP-A does not dictate the internal design of Vehicle or Teleoperation; it only consumes their outputs.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: All modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) emit telemetry events with consistent timestamps and root-cause classifications. Risk: If modules use different time sources or classification schemes, availability calculations will be unreliable. Mitigation: establish a telemetry standard and enforce it via interface contracts.

Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms (e.g., remaining useful life estimation) are sufficiently accurate to reduce unplanned downtime. Risk: If predictions are poor, maintenance will be scheduled unnecessarily, increasing costs and downtime. Mitigation: validate predictive models against historical failure data; start with conservative thresholds.

Assumption: Site Manager and Maintenance personnel will act on availability alerts and maintenance recommendations. Risk: If alerts are ignored or maintenance is deferred, availability will not improve. Mitigation: integrate alerts into the Site Manager's operational dashboard; make maintenance recommendations visible and actionable.

Assumption: Safety-driven shutdowns are rare and do not dominate the availability calculation. Risk: If safety shutdowns are frequent, the system may be unsafe or the safety logic may be overly conservative. Mitigation: monitor the frequency and duration of safety shutdowns; escalate to Safety Manager if trends indicate a systemic issue.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-A is sound but should be refined to explicitly address the distinction between safety-driven and failure-driven unavailability. Recommend adding a sub-DP: 'DP-A.1: Implement a safety-shutdown classifier that tags all SafetyAlert events as either safety-critical (unavailability is acceptable) or safety-advisory (unavailability should be minimized). Use this classification to compute two availability metrics: (1) overall availability (including safety shutdowns), and (2) failure-driven availability (excluding safety shutdowns). Report both to stakeholders to provide transparency on the sources of unavailability.'

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary. It owns the availability monitoring and reporting subsystem, which aggregates telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. The subsystem includes: (1) a telemetry aggregation engine that collects and time-synchronizes events from all modules, (2) an availability calculation engine that computes real-time and historical availability metrics, (3) a predictive maintenance engine that analyzes component health trends, (4) an alerting and reporting service that notifies Site Manager and Maintenance personnel, and (5) a safety-shutdown classifier that distinguishes safety-driven from failure-driven unavailability.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives timestamped health events from Vehicle (motor health, hydraulic pressure, structural integrity), Teleoperation (network latency, control signal loss, operator connection status), and Safety Services (safety sensor readings, safety-driven shutdown events). Schema: {timestamp, source_module, event_type, severity, root_cause_code, component_id, metric_value}.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical shutdown events from Safety Services. Used to classify unavailability as safety-driven vs. failure-driven. Schema: {timestamp, alert_type, severity, safety_classification, component_affected, recommended_action}.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends maintenance task recommendations to Maintenance/Quality personnel. Schema: {task_id, component_id, recommended_action, urgency, estimated_duration, predicted_failure_time}.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the availability monitoring subsystem because: (1) it is the top-level system integration point, (2) it has visibility into all sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), (3) availability is a system-level KPI, not a module-level concern, (4) the subsystem must aggregate data from multiple modules, which is a system-level responsibility. Diffusion risk is minimal because the subsystem is cohesive and does not duplicate logic across modules.

Vehicle module should NOT own the availability monitoring subsystem because: (1) availability is a system-level KPI, not a vehicle-level concern, (2) Vehicle does not have visibility into Teleoperation or Safety Services, (3) placing the subsystem in Vehicle would require Vehicle to import and depend on interfaces from other modules, creating tight coupling and circular dependencies, (4) Vehicle's primary responsibility is to execute mobility and manipulation tasks, not to monitor system-level availability. If Vehicle owned this subsystem, the logic would be diffused and difficult to maintain.

Teleoperation module should NOT own the availability monitoring subsystem because: (1) Teleoperation is responsible for remote control and situational awareness, not system-level availability management, (2) Teleoperation does not have visibility into Vehicle or Safety Services health, (3) placing the subsystem in Teleoperation would create an unnatural dependency on Vehicle and Safety Services modules, (4) Teleoperation's primary responsibility is to enable remote operation, not to monitor overall system availability. Containment score is very low.

Safety Services module should NOT own the availability monitoring subsystem because: (1) Safety Services is responsible for safety-critical monitoring and shutdown logic, not availability management, (2) Safety Services should not be burdened with the responsibility of aggregating telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation, (3) placing the subsystem in Safety Services would blur the boundary between safety-critical and non-safety-critical logic, (4) Safety Services should emit SafetyAlert events that the availability subsystem consumes, not own the subsystem itself. However, Safety Services should provide a clear SafetyAlert interface that the availability subsystem can use to classify shutdowns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The availability monitoring and reporting subsystem is best contained within the NGHE Solution module because: (1) it is a system-level concern that requires visibility into all sub-modules, (2) it aggregates data from multiple sources (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), (3) it computes a system-level KPI (availability), (4) it does not duplicate logic across modules, (5) it has clear, well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert in, Tasking out). The subsystem is cohesive and does not have diffusion risks.
containment_risks: Risk: If the Telemetry interface is not standardized across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, the availability subsystem will receive inconsistent data and produce unreliable metrics. Mitigation: define a strict telemetry schema and enforce it via interface contracts. Conduct integration testing to verify that all modules emit telemetry in the expected format.

Risk: If the availability subsystem becomes too complex (e.g., if predictive maintenance algorithms are added), it may become difficult to maintain and test. Mitigation: decompose the subsystem into smaller, testable components (telemetry aggregation, availability calculation, predictive maintenance, alerting). Use a modular architecture (e.g., microservices or plugins) to allow independent evolution of each component.

Risk: If the availability subsystem depends on real-time telemetry from all modules, a failure in any module's telemetry pipeline will cause the availability subsystem to fail or produce incomplete data. Mitigation: implement graceful degradation in the availability subsystem; if telemetry from one module is unavailable, continue calculating availability based on available data and flag the gap in the report.

Risk: If the availability subsystem is tightly coupled to the Tasking interface (maintenance task management), changes to the maintenance workflow may require changes to the availability subsystem. Mitigation: define the Tasking interface as a contract; use a message queue or event bus to decouple the availability subsystem from the maintenance task management system.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: The IM assignment is sound. Recommend the following refinements: (1) Define a formal Telemetry schema (JSON or protobuf) that all modules must conform to. Include fields for timestamp, source_module, event_type, severity, root_cause_code, component_id, and metric_value. (2) Define a formal SafetyAlert schema that Safety Services must emit. Include fields for timestamp, alert_type, severity, safety_classification (safety-critical vs. safety-advisory), component_affected, and recommended_action. (3) Implement the availability subsystem as a set of loosely-coupled microservices: telemetry aggregation service, availability calculation service, predictive maintenance service, and alerting service. Use a message queue (e.g., RabbitMQ, Kafka) to decouple services. (4) Establish a clear ownership boundary: NGHE Solution owns the availability subsystem; Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own their respective telemetry and alert producers.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (Centralized Availability Monitoring & Predictive Maintenance) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach: aggregate telemetry from all modules, calculate availability metrics, distinguish safety-driven from failure-driven unavailability, and trigger predictive maintenance recommendations. The DP is measurable (availability is calculated as a percentage), verifiable (telemetry logs can be audited), and achievable (similar systems exist in industry). The DP does not over-constrain the design; it allows flexibility in the implementation details (e.g., choice of predictive maintenance algorithm, alerting mechanism, UI design).

diffusion_risks: Risk: If the availability subsystem is implemented as a monolithic component within NGHE Solution, it may become difficult to test and maintain as the system grows. Mitigation: decompose the subsystem into smaller, testable microservices.

Risk: If the Telemetry interface is not standardized, different modules may emit telemetry in different formats, causing the availability subsystem to fail or produce unreliable metrics. Mitigation: define a strict telemetry schema and enforce it via interface contracts.

Risk: If the availability subsystem depends on real-time telemetry from all modules, a failure in any module's telemetry pipeline will cause the availability subsystem to fail. Mitigation: implement graceful degradation; if telemetry from one module is unavailable, continue calculating availability based on available data.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The availability subsystem is well-contained within NGHE Solution. It has clear external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert in, Tasking out) and does not duplicate logic across modules. The subsystem is cohesive and does not have diffusion risks, provided that the Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces are standardized and enforced.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-004 is feasible. The availability metric is objective and measurable: (Total Scheduled Time − Unplanned Downtime) / Total Scheduled Time. Verification requires: (1) collection of operational telemetry from deployed NGHE units over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (2) calculation of availability metrics from telemetry logs, (3) independent audit of telemetry logs and maintenance records to verify accuracy, (4) stratification by failure mode to identify improvement priorities. The NGHE Solution module (via the availability subsystem) is responsible for collecting and reporting telemetry; Maintenance/Quality is responsible for auditing the data.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of FR-NGHE-004 to NGHE Solution is clear. NGHE Solution owns the availability monitoring and reporting subsystem, which is responsible for: (1) aggregating telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, (2) calculating availability metrics, (3) distinguishing safety-driven from failure-driven unavailability, (4) triggering predictive maintenance recommendations, (5) providing real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance personnel. The sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are allocated to specific modules: FR-NGHE-004.1 (event detection and logging) is allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; FR-NGHE-004.2 (minimize downtime) is allocated to Vehicle and Teleoperation; FR-NGHE-004.3 (availability visibility) is allocated to NGHE Solution; FR-NGHE-004.4 (safety-driven unavailability classification) is allocated to NGHE Solution and Safety Services.

recommended_revisions: Refine the Telemetry interface to include a standardized schema that all modules must conform to. Include fields for timestamp, source_module, event_type, severity, root_cause_code, component_id, and metric_value.

Refine the SafetyAlert interface to include a safety_classification field that distinguishes safety-critical (unavailability is acceptable) from safety-advisory (unavailability should be minimized) events.
Decompose the availability subsystem into smaller, testable microservices: telemetry aggregation, availability calculation, predictive maintenance, and alerting.
Establish a clear ownership boundary: NGHE Solution owns the availability subsystem; Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own their respective telemetry and alert producers.

Define acceptance criteria for the availability KPI: (1) availability is calculated as (Total Scheduled Time − Unplanned Downtime) / Total Scheduled Time, (2) unplanned downtime excludes planned maintenance and safety-driven shutdowns, (3) availability is measured over a rolling 30/90-day window, (4) availability is reported daily to Site Manager and Maintenance personnel, (5) predictive maintenance recommendations are generated when component health indicators predict imminent failure.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into sub-FRs to be actionable. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the IM assignment to NGHE Solution is appropriate. However, the sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) require further decomposition to allocate responsibilities to specific modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and to define the detailed design of the availability subsystem. Specifically: (1) FR-NGHE-004.1 (event detection and logging) must be decomposed into module-specific requirements for Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, (2) FR-NGHE-004.2 (minimize downtime) must be decomposed into specific design parameters for redundancy, fault recovery, and predictive maintenance, (3) FR-NGHE-004.3 (availability visibility) must be decomposed into UI/UX requirements for the Site Manager dashboard, (4) FR-NGHE-004.4 (safety-driven unavailability classification) must be decomposed into specific logic for distinguishing safety-critical from safety-advisory events. Proceeding to the next level of decomposition will clarify the design and enable detailed implementation planning.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all availability-affecting events (failures, maintenance, safety shutdowns) with timestamp and root cause classification.

FR-NGHE-004.1.1: The Vehicle module shall emit telemetry events for mechanical failures (motor overload, hydraulic pressure loss, structural damage), electrical failures (sensor malfunction, control signal loss), and maintenance events (scheduled maintenance, component replacement) with timestamp, event type, severity, root cause code, and component ID.

FR-NGHE-004.1.2: The Teleoperation module shall emit telemetry events for network failures (connection loss, latency exceeding threshold), control signal failures (operator input loss, command timeout), and operator connection events (operator login/logout, session timeout) with timestamp, event type, severity, and component ID.

FR-NGHE-004.1.3: The Safety Services module shall emit SafetyAlert events for safety-critical shutdowns (emergency stop, safety sensor activation, safety logic timeout) and safety-advisory alerts (safety sensor degradation, safety logic warning) with timestamp, alert type, severity, safety classification, component affected, and recommended action.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance, redundancy, and rapid fault recovery mechanisms.

FR-NGHE-004.2.1: The Vehicle module shall implement redundancy for critical subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual hydraulic pumps) to enable graceful degradation and continued operation in degraded mode.
FR-NGHE-004.2.2: The Vehicle module shall implement rapid fault recovery mechanisms (automatic failover, self-healing algorithms, component reset logic) to minimize downtime when failures occur.

FR-NGHE-004.2.3: The NGHE Solution shall implement predictive maintenance algorithms that analyze component health trends (vibration, temperature, pressure, electrical current) to predict imminent failures and recommend preventive maintenance before failures occur.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance personnel.

FR-NGHE-004.3.1: The NGHE Solution shall provide a Site Manager dashboard that displays real-time availability status (percentage), availability trend (30-day rolling average), current downtime events (if any), and recommended maintenance actions.

FR-NGHE-004.3.2: The NGHE Solution shall provide a Maintenance portal that displays component health status, predictive maintenance recommendations, maintenance history, and maintenance task management interface.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain safety-driven unavailability as a distinct category, not penalizing availability KPI when safety shutdowns occur.

FR-NGHE-004.4.1: The NGHE Solution shall classify all SafetyAlert events as either safety-critical (unavailability is acceptable and does not penalize availability KPI) or safety-advisory (unavailability should be minimized and may penalize availability KPI).

FR-NGHE-004.4.2: The NGHE Solution shall compute two availability metrics: (1) overall availability (including all unavailability events), and (2) failure-driven availability (excluding safety-critical unavailability events). Report both metrics to stakeholders to provide transparency on the sources of unavailability.

assumptions: Assumption: All modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) emit telemetry events with consistent timestamps and root-cause classifications. This is critical for accurate availability calculation.

Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms are sufficiently accurate to reduce unplanned downtime. If predictions are poor, maintenance will be scheduled unnecessarily, increasing costs and downtime.
Assumption: Site Manager and Maintenance personnel will act on availability alerts and maintenance recommendations. If alerts are ignored or maintenance is deferred, availability will not improve.

Assumption: Safety-driven shutdowns are rare and do not dominate the availability calculation. If safety shutdowns are frequent, the system may be unsafe or the safety logic may be overly conservative.
Assumption: The customer-defined availability target is ≥0.97 (97%). If the customer defines a different target, the design parameters may need to be adjusted.
Assumption: The evaluation period for availability measurement is agreed upon with the customer (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year). Different evaluation periods may yield different results.
open_risks: Risk: If the Telemetry interface is not standardized across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, the availability subsystem will receive inconsistent data and produce unreliable metrics. Mitigation: define a strict telemetry schema and enforce it via interface contracts.
Risk: If the availability subsystem becomes too complex, it may become difficult to maintain and test. Mitigation: decompose the subsystem into smaller, testable microservices.

Risk: If the availability subsystem depends on real-time telemetry from all modules, a failure in any module's telemetry pipeline will cause the availability subsystem to fail. Mitigation: implement graceful degradation.

Risk: If predictive maintenance algorithms are not validated against historical failure data, they may produce unreliable recommendations. Mitigation: validate models against historical data; start with conservative thresholds.

Risk: If Site Manager and Maintenance personnel do not have clear visibility into availability metrics and maintenance recommendations, they may not take appropriate action. Mitigation: provide a clear, intuitive dashboard and maintenance portal.

Risk: If safety-driven shutdowns are frequent, the system may be unsafe or the safety logic may be overly conservative. Mitigation: monitor the frequency and duration of safety shutdowns; escalate to Safety Manager if trends indicate a systemic issue.
evidence_needed: Evidence: Telemetry schema definition (JSON or protobuf) that all modules must conform to. Include fields for timestamp, source_module, event_type, severity, root_cause_code, component_id, and metric_value.
Evidence: SafetyAlert schema definition that Safety Services must emit. Include fields for timestamp, alert_type, severity, safety_classification, component_affected, and recommended_action.
Evidence: Historical failure data from similar NGHE systems (or competitor systems) to validate predictive maintenance algorithms.

Evidence: Acceptance criteria for the availability KPI: (1) availability is calculated as (Total Scheduled Time − Unplanned Downtime) / Total Scheduled Time, (2) unplanned downtime excludes planned maintenance and safety-driven shutdowns, (3) availability is measured over a rolling 30/90-day window, (4) availability is reported daily to Site Manager and Maintenance personnel.
Evidence: Design specification for the availability subsystem, including architecture (microservices vs. monolithic), component interfaces, and data flow.
Evidence: Design specification for the Site Manager dashboard and Maintenance portal, including UI mockups, user workflows, and acceptance criteria.

Evidence: Test plan for the availability subsystem, including unit tests, integration tests, and system tests. Include test cases for edge cases (e.g., telemetry loss, safety shutdown classification, predictive maintenance accuracy).
Evidence: Deployment plan for the availability subsystem, including rollout strategy, monitoring, and rollback procedures.
